title: France's Caribbean Empire
summary: The Treaty of Ryswick in 1697 gave France formal control of Saint-Domingue — the western third of Hispaniola, modern Haiti. Over the following century, France developed it into the most profitable colony on Earth. By the 1780s, Saint-Domingue produced roughly 40% of Europe's sugar and more than half of its coffee, generated more trade revenue than all thirteen American colonies combined, and was worked by nearly half a million enslaved people. The colony's extraordinary wealth made it the centrepiece of the French Empire — and made the question of who controlled it a matter of existential importance to Napoleon.

title: Slavery as Economic Foundation
summary: Saint-Domingue's wealth was built entirely on enslaved labour under conditions of extreme brutality. Mortality rates were so high that the colony required constant importation of new enslaved people from West Africa — an estimated 40,000 per year by the 1780s — simply to maintain the workforce. This demographic reality created a society of radical inequality: 500,000 enslaved people, 30,000 free people of colour, and 30,000 white colonists. It was a society that functioned only through terror, and that carried within it the conditions of its own violent destruction.

title: French Revolution Reaches the Caribbean
summary: The French Revolution's declaration that all men are born free and equal created an immediate and unresolvable contradiction in a colony built on slavery. Free people of colour in Saint-Domingue immediately demanded equal rights; the white colonists refused; Paris vacillated. A free man of colour named Vincent Ogé led an uprising in 1790 demanding political rights and was executed. His death radicalised the free coloured population. When news of the revolution's principles reached the enslaved people of the plantations, the contradiction became explosive.

title: Napoleon's European Ambitions
summary: Napoleon Bonaparte seized power in France in November 1799 with continental ambitions that required enormous and continuous military expenditure. His vision of a French empire in the Americas — with Louisiana as a breadbasket supplying Saint-Domingue, and Saint-Domingue generating the wealth to fund European campaigns — depended on first restoring French control of the Caribbean colony. When that plan collapsed, the entire American strategy became not just undesirable but actively dangerous: territory France couldn't defend would simply fall to Britain.

title: British Naval Supremacy
summary: Britain's Royal Navy was the most powerful maritime force in the world, and from 1793 onward it was at war with France almost continuously. British naval dominance meant that any French territory in the Americas was strategically indefensible: supply lines could be cut, reinforcements blocked, and ports blockaded at will. Napoleon understood that Louisiana — vast, landlocked, and separated from France by an ocean his navy couldn't control — was a gift he was holding for Britain. The sea he couldn't cross made the land he couldn't keep worthless.

title: Saint-Domingue — The Pearl of the Antilles
summary: By the 1780s, Saint-Domingue was the most valuable piece of real estate on Earth. Its sugar, coffee, cotton, and indigo made it responsible for roughly a third of France's total foreign trade. Merchants in Bordeaux and Nantes had built their fortunes on it; French manufacturing supplied its plantations; French banks financed its slave trade. The colony was so central to the French economy that losing it was unthinkable — which is precisely why Napoleon sent an army of 40,000 men to take it back, and why their failure was so consequential.

title: French Economy Dependent on Saint-Domingue
summary: Saint-Domingue's dominance in the French economy created a political logic that made military reconquest feel unavoidable when the revolution broke out. Merchants, planters, and financiers lobbied furiously for Napoleon to restore order. The colony represented not just current revenue but the entire financial rationale for France's American empire. Losing Saint-Domingue meant losing Louisiana's purpose — the breadbasket territory was meaningless without the profitable colony it was meant to supply.

title: Haitian Revolution Begins
summary: On the night of August 22, 1791, enslaved people across Saint-Domingue's northern plain rose in a coordinated revolt — burning plantations, killing enslavers, and seizing the countryside in what became the only successful slave revolution in history. The uprising had been planned at a secret ceremony at Bois Caïman days earlier. Within weeks, the northern province was in flames. The French government, distracted by revolution at home, sent contradictory orders; the colonists armed themselves. What began as an uprising would become a twelve-year war for independence that shocked every slaveholding society on Earth.

title: Toussaint L'Ouverture Takes Command
summary: Toussaint L'Ouverture — a formerly enslaved man who had been freed before the revolution — emerged as the dominant military and political leader of the uprising. A self-taught military genius who had read Caesar and studied European tactics, he transformed a peasant revolt into a disciplined army, played French, Spanish, and British forces against each other, and by 1801 had effective control of the entire island and drafted its own constitution. Napoleon viewed him as an intolerable precedent — a Black general governing a French colony on his own terms — and sent an army to remove him.

title: Napoleon Sends an Army
summary: Napoleon dispatched his brother-in-law General Leclerc with 40,000 troops — the largest French expeditionary force ever sent to the Americas — to restore French control of Saint-Domingue and re-establish slavery. Leclerc captured Toussaint L'Ouverture through treachery (inviting him to a meeting and arresting him), who died in a French prison in 1803. But the revolution continued without him under Jean-Jacques Dessalines and Henri Christophe. And then the disease arrived.

title: Yellow Fever Destroys the French Army
summary: Yellow fever — endemic in the Caribbean and against which locals had significant immunity — tore through the French expeditionary force with catastrophic efficiency. Leclerc himself died of it in November 1802. Of the roughly 40,000 soldiers Napoleon sent, an estimated 50,000–80,000 French troops died in Saint-Domingue over the course of the war — the vast majority from disease rather than combat. The army that was supposed to restore France's American empire had been destroyed by a mosquito. Napoleon, who had planned to use Saint-Domingue as the anchor of a continental American empire, was left with nothing.

title: Napoleon Abandons the Americas
summary: By late 1802, Napoleon had lost his army, lost his general, and lost his nerve for the Americas. He reportedly told his finance minister Barbé-Marbois: 'Damn sugar, damn coffee, damn colonies.' With the Haitian revolution clearly winning, Britain poised to resume war, and the Royal Navy capable of intercepting any ship he sent westward, Louisiana was now a liability rather than an asset. Holding it would require garrisoning it, which required a fleet he didn't have. Selling it would fund the European campaigns he actually cared about. The decision was made in weeks.

title: Napoleon Needs Money for War
summary: The renewed war with Britain — the Peace of Amiens had collapsed by 1803 — required immediate and massive military expenditure. Napoleon's treasury was stretched. The Louisiana Territory, which he had acquired from Spain only in 1800 and never effectively administered, offered a way to convert a strategic liability into ready cash. American negotiators Robert Livingston and James Monroe had been authorised to offer $10 million for New Orleans alone; Napoleon's finance minister offered them the entire territory for $15 million. The Americans, stunned, accepted before Paris could change its mind.

title: Louisiana Purchase Signed
summary: On April 30, 1803, the United States purchased approximately 828,000 square miles of territory from France for $15 million — roughly three cents per acre. The transaction doubled the size of the United States overnight, adding all or part of fifteen future states from Louisiana to Montana. It was the largest peaceful land transaction in history. Napoleon got his war chest. Jefferson got a continent. The Haitian revolutionaries, whose victory had made it possible, got nothing — and would shortly be made to pay for it.

title: Haiti Declares Independence
summary: On January 1, 1804, Jean-Jacques Dessalines declared the independent Republic of Haiti — the first Black republic in history and the second independent nation in the Western Hemisphere after the United States. The name Haiti came from the indigenous Taíno word for the island. The declaration was a world-historical event: the only successful slave revolution had produced an independent state. The United States, itself a slaveholding republic, refused to recognise Haiti for 58 years, terrified of the precedent. Thomas Jefferson, who had just completed the Louisiana Purchase, imposed a trade embargo.

title: Haiti Isolated and Indebted
summary: In 1825, France agreed to recognise Haitian independence — on condition that Haiti pay 150 million francs in reparations to compensate former slaveholders for their 'lost property,' including the people Haiti had freed. Haiti agreed, having no real choice: French warships sat in the harbour during the negotiations. Haiti took out loans to pay this debt, which was not fully repaid until 1947. The country that had made the Louisiana Purchase possible by defeating Napoleon's army was simultaneously embargoed by the United States and indebted to France for the crime of winning its own freedom.

title: United States Doubles in Size
summary: The Louisiana Purchase instantly transformed the United States from a coastal republic hugging the Atlantic seaboard into a continental power with a western frontier stretching to the Rocky Mountains. The Mississippi River — previously a contested border — became an interior highway. New Orleans, controlling access to the river's entire drainage basin, became an American city. The psychological effect was as significant as the geographical one: for the first time, American leaders could imagine a nation stretching to the Pacific, and the ideology to justify that expansion would not be long in forming.

title: Jefferson's Constitutional Crisis
summary: Thomas Jefferson, the strictest strict-constructionist in American politics, faced an acute problem: the Constitution gave the president no explicit power to purchase foreign territory, let alone incorporate it into the republic. He initially drafted a constitutional amendment but abandoned it when he learned Napoleon might withdraw the offer. Jefferson swallowed his principles and submitted the treaty to the Senate, which ratified it overwhelmingly. The precedent was enormous: the federal government had just established that it could acquire and govern territory beyond the original states, dramatically expanding executive and federal power in ways Jefferson's own political philosophy opposed.

title: Federal Power Expands
summary: The Louisiana Purchase established a constitutional precedent that the federal government could acquire, govern, and eventually admit as states enormous territories never contemplated by the founders. This expansionist interpretation of federal power became the legal and political framework for every subsequent territorial acquisition — Florida, Texas, the Mexican cession, Alaska, Hawaii. The strict-constructionist who bought Louisiana had inadvertently created the legal infrastructure for a continental empire.

title: Lewis and Clark Expedition
summary: Jefferson dispatched Meriwether Lewis and William Clark in 1804 to explore the new territory and find a route to the Pacific — accompanied by the Shoshone woman Sacagawea, whose knowledge and diplomatic skills proved essential. The Corps of Discovery returned in 1806 with detailed maps, botanical specimens, ethnographic records of dozens of Native nations, and proof that a land route to the Pacific existed. Their journals, widely published, transformed the American West from abstract geography into a place people could imagine reaching. The expedition's maps became the template for every subsequent wagon trail.

title: New Territory Reopens Slavery Question
summary: The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 had banned slavery north of the Ohio River — but Louisiana was west of everything, in territory the ordinance didn't cover. The question of whether the new states carved from the purchase would be slave or free immediately fractured American politics along sectional lines. Southern slaveholders insisted on the right to bring enslaved people into new territories; Northern free-soilers resisted. The Louisiana Purchase didn't create the slavery crisis — it vastly enlarged the battlefield on which it would be fought.

title: The West Mapped and Known
summary: Lewis and Clark's maps and journals, combined with subsequent expeditions by Zebulon Pike and John Frémont, gave Americans practical knowledge of western routes, river systems, mountain passes, and fertile valleys. By the 1830s, fur traders had established the Oregon Trail as a viable wagon route to the Pacific Northwest. Knowledge transformed aspiration into practicality: settlers could now move west with detailed guidance on where to go, what to expect, and how to survive. The West stopped being mythological and became logistical.

title: Pressure on Native Nations Intensifies
summary: The Louisiana Purchase placed the United States government as the sovereign authority over territory home to dozens of Native nations — the Osage, Sioux, Mandan, Arikara, and many others — who had received no notice and given no consent. American settlers, fur traders, and military expeditions immediately began moving into these territories. The federal government's new constitutional power to govern acquired territory meant it now had both the authority and the incentive to remove Native peoples from land that American settlers wanted. The Purchase didn't initiate this pressure but dramatically expanded its geographic scope.

title: Indian Removal Act
summary: President Andrew Jackson signed the Indian Removal Act in 1830, authorising the forced relocation of Native nations from east of the Mississippi to territory west of it — land that had itself been part of the Louisiana Purchase. The act was presented as voluntary but enforced through economic pressure, treaty manipulation, and military force. It affected the Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, and Seminole nations primarily, displacing tens of thousands of people from ancestral homelands. Jackson's policy made explicit what the Louisiana Purchase had made possible: the continent was to be cleared for American settlement.

title: Trail of Tears
summary: The forced relocation of the Cherokee nation in 1838–1839 — known as Nunna daul Tsuny, 'the trail where they cried' — killed an estimated 4,000–8,000 of the roughly 16,000 people marched westward in winter conditions, without adequate food, clothing, or shelter. Similar forced marches affected the other 'Five Civilised Tribes.' The Trail of Tears became the defining emblem of American Indian policy: the systematic dispossession of Native peoples to make room for westward expansion, carried out by a government that had declared itself founded on the principle that all men are created equal.

title: Manifest Destiny
summary: Journalist John O'Sullivan coined the phrase 'manifest destiny' in 1845 — the belief that American expansion across the continent was not merely desirable but divinely ordained and historically inevitable. The ideology gave moral and theological cover to what was in practice a programme of territorial acquisition, Native dispossession, and military conquest. It justified the annexation of Texas (1845), the Mexican-American War (1846–48), and the Oregon settlement with Britain (1846). Manifest Destiny was less a cause of American expansion than its retrospective justification — the ideology that made what had already been done feel inevitable.

title: Missouri Compromise
summary: Missouri's application for statehood in 1819 — as a slave state carved from the Louisiana Purchase — triggered a two-year congressional crisis that Thomas Jefferson called 'a fire bell in the night.' The Missouri Compromise of 1820 admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, and drew a line at 36°30' latitude dividing future slave from free territory. It resolved the immediate crisis but established a formula — balance between slave and free states — that made every subsequent territorial acquisition a sectional political battle. The Louisiana Purchase had doubled the country's size and halved its political stability.

title: Mexican-American War
summary: The United States declared war on Mexico in May 1846 following a disputed border skirmish in Texas, which the US had annexed the previous year. In practice the war was a land grab on an enormous scale: the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848) transferred California, Nevada, Utah, most of Arizona, and parts of New Mexico, Colorado, and Wyoming to the United States — over 500,000 square miles — in exchange for $15 million (the same price as Louisiana). Critics including Abraham Lincoln and Ulysses S. Grant called it an unjust war of aggression. The territory seized reignited the slavery debate with a fury the Missouri Compromise could no longer contain.

title: Continental United States Takes Shape
summary: By 1848, the United States extended from the Atlantic to the Pacific — a transformation achieved in 45 years through purchase, treaty, and conquest. The Louisiana Purchase had started the process; the Mexican cession completed the continental outline. What had been a coastal republic of 13 states was now a continental empire of over 3 million square miles, built on the dispossession of Native nations, the labour of enslaved people, and the military defeat of Mexico. The question of what kind of country would govern this continent — slave or free, expansionist or republican — was about to tear it apart.

title: Gadsden Purchase Completes the Map
summary: The United States purchased a strip of land from Mexico in 1853 — modern southern Arizona and New Mexico — for $10 million, primarily to secure a southern route for a transcontinental railroad. The Gadsden Purchase completed the contiguous territorial outline of the continental United States as it exists today. The nation that had been a strip of Atlantic seaboard in 1800 now spanned a continent. The process had taken 50 years, three major land transactions, one war, and the forced removal of dozens of Native nations. The Louisiana Purchase, sold by a man who needed money for a European war, had made all of it possible.

title: Spain Controls Louisiana
summary: France secretly ceded Louisiana to Spain via the Treaty of Fontainebleau in 1762, confirmed publicly by the Treaty of Paris in 1763, as compensation for Spain's losses in the Seven Years' War. Spain governed Louisiana for nearly four decades — a largely neglected backwater generating little revenue but serving as a buffer between British North America and Spanish Mexico. When Napoleon Bonaparte rose to power, Louisiana's strategic position between the United States and Spanish silver mines made it suddenly valuable again, and he moved to recover it.

title: Treaty of San Ildefonso
summary: Napoleon pressured the weakened Spanish Crown to secretly retrocede Louisiana to France in the Treaty of San Ildefonso, signed October 1, 1800. Spain agreed in exchange for Napoleon's promise to create a kingdom in Italy for the Spanish king's son-in-law — a promise he never fully kept. The treaty was kept secret for over a year; when American intelligence confirmed it in 1801, it alarmed Jefferson enormously. France replacing Spain as the power controlling the Mississippi's mouth meant a far more capable adversary now held the key to American western commerce.

title: Napoleon's American Empire Vision
summary: Napoleon conceived a grandiose American empire: Saint-Domingue would be restored as the productive engine, Louisiana would serve as its breadbasket and supplier, and together they would create a French presence in the Americas powerful enough to rival Britain and contain the growing United States. He wrote that New Orleans would become 'the city most important in the world.' The plan depended entirely on first crushing the Haitian Revolution — and it was the failure of that prerequisite that unravelled everything. Without Saint-Domingue, Louisiana had no purpose.

title: America's Dependence on the Mississippi
summary: Pinckney's Treaty of 1795 secured American farmers the right of deposit at New Orleans — the ability to offload goods onto ocean-going ships without paying Spanish duties. For settlers in Kentucky, Ohio, and Tennessee, the Mississippi River was the only practical route to market: goods travelled downstream to New Orleans, then by sea to eastern cities or Europe. When Spain threatened this access, western Americans faced economic ruin. Control of New Orleans was not a strategic abstraction to Jefferson — it was the economic lifeline of half the country.

title: New Orleans Right of Deposit Revoked
summary: In October 1802, the Spanish intendant at New Orleans revoked the American right of deposit — possibly on French instructions, possibly acting independently. Western American farmers suddenly could not legally offload their goods. The political reaction was explosive: congressmen from western states demanded war; newspapers called for seizing New Orleans by force. Jefferson, facing a domestic crisis he could not ignore, accelerated diplomatic efforts to purchase New Orleans before the situation forced his hand militarily. The crisis transformed a desirable acquisition into an urgent one.

title: Jefferson Tries to Buy New Orleans
summary: Jefferson dispatched James Monroe to join ambassador Robert Livingston in Paris in early 1803, authorised to offer up to $10 million for New Orleans and West Florida — just the city and a strip of Gulf Coast. Neither man expected what happened next: French Finance Minister Barbé-Marbois, acting on Napoleon's instructions, offered the entire Louisiana Territory for $15 million. Livingston and Monroe had no authorisation to accept an offer of this scale, and no time to consult Washington. They signed the treaty anyway, betting their careers and reputations on Jefferson's approval.

title: New Orleans Becomes America's Port
summary: With New Orleans under American control, the entire Mississippi-Missouri river system — draining 40% of the continent — became an American commercial highway. New Orleans grew from a modest colonial town into the fourth-largest city in the United States by 1840, and for decades the busiest port in the country by tonnage. Cotton, tobacco, sugar, and grain from the interior flowed through it to the world. The city's wealth was built on enslaved labour and the slave trade — the same economic forces that made the Louisiana Territory so valuable also concentrated the brutality of American slavery into a single explosive location.

title: Steamboats Transform the Mississippi
summary: The first steamboat to navigate the Mississippi — aptly named the New Orleans — completed its voyage from Pittsburgh in 1811. Within a decade, steam-powered vessels had transformed river commerce: goods could now travel upstream as easily as down, journey times collapsed, and freight costs fell dramatically. By the 1830s, hundreds of steamboats worked the western rivers. The economic integration of the Louisiana Purchase territory depended on this technology — without steamboats, the vast interior would have remained economically isolated. Mark Twain's Mississippi was a steamboat world made possible by the Purchase.

title: Cotton Kingdom Expands West
summary: The Louisiana Purchase opened some of the most fertile cotton-growing land in North America. Mississippi achieved statehood in 1817, Alabama in 1819, Arkansas in 1836, Louisiana itself in 1812 — all carved from the purchase territory, all slave states. By the 1830s, the Lower Mississippi Valley had become the heart of the American cotton economy, producing the raw material that supplied British and New England textile mills and making slavery not merely a Southern institution but the engine of the entire Atlantic economy. The purchase that Jefferson hoped would secure a republic of yeoman farmers instead entrenched a slave empire.

title: New Orleans: Largest Slave Market in America
summary: The US ban on importing enslaved people from Africa (effective 1808) paradoxically intensified the domestic slave trade: with international supply cut off, the internal market became the only legal source of enslaved labour for the expanding cotton states. New Orleans became the largest slave market in North America — a city where enslaved people were bought and sold more efficiently than anywhere else on the continent. The phrase 'sold down the river' entered the American language as a description of the terror enslaved people in the Upper South lived with: separation from family and shipment to the brutal cotton and sugar plantations of the Deep South. Between 1820 and 1860, an estimated one million enslaved people were sold in this internal trade.

title: Slavery Crisis Deepens
summary: The expansion of cotton slavery into Louisiana Purchase territory transformed the slavery debate from a manageable political irritant into an existential national crisis. The abolitionist movement grew in direct response to the cotton kingdom's expansion and the internal slave trade's visibility. William Lloyd Garrison founded The Liberator in 1831; Nat Turner's rebellion the same year terrified the South into hardening its defence of slavery. Each new territory acquired raised the same question with greater urgency: slave or free? The Missouri Compromise line held for a generation — but the pressure building beneath it would eventually produce the Civil War.

title: Texas Annexed
summary: Texas had declared independence from Mexico in 1836 and existed as an independent republic for nine years before American annexation in December 1845. The delay reflected the slavery question: Texas would enter as an enormous slave state, upsetting the balance the Missouri Compromise had maintained. Southern politicians pushed hard for annexation; Northern Whigs resisted. President Polk's successful annexation immediately provoked Mexico, which had never recognised Texan independence, and provided the pretext for the Mexican-American War that followed within months. Every acre of Texas had ultimately become American because of the expansionist appetite the Louisiana Purchase had awakened.

title: California Gold Rush
summary: Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in the Sierra Nevada on January 24, 1848 — nine days before the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo formally transferred California to the United States. Within two years, 100,000 people had flooded into California from across the world — the largest voluntary mass migration in American history to that point. California achieved statehood in 1850 as a free state, immediately reigniting the slavery crisis the Missouri Compromise had suppressed. The Gold Rush accelerated the transcontinental railroad, the destruction of California's Native peoples, and the economic integration of the Pacific coast — all consequences flowing from a war fought over land that Napoleon had sold to fund his European ambitions.
